question: Co-infective Prevalence: What is the proportion of TB patients who are also HIV/AIDS positive, and how does this impact management protocols?
prompt: I need a complete Python analysis of a healthcare dataset (a CSV file named 'Table1') from a Telemedicine project. The analysis needs to be divided into three main parts, each generating a specific visualization, printing a console report, and exporting the underlying data to a CSV file. Please use pandas, numpy, matplotlib, seaborn, and plotly. Part 1: TB and HIV Co-infection Analysis. Data Processing: Create a binarized status for Tuberculosis (TB) and HIV. You need to use both text mining (searching for terms like 'tuberculose', 'tb', 'hiv', 'aids' in free-text columns) and structured data (looking for checked boxes in columns related to infectious diseases or CIAP codes A70 for TB and B90 for HIV). Aggregation: Aggregate the data so that if a patient (identified by 'Record ID') ever had a positive mark for TB or HIV across any of their consultations, they are considered positive for that condition. Output 1 (Export): Export this patient-level binarized table (Record ID, TB Status, HIV Status) to a CSV named 'Tabela_Bruta_TB_HIV_TeleSAP.csv'. Visualization: Create a bar chart showing the prevalence of HIV (%) in two groups: patients without TB and patients with TB. Use a specific color palette (e.g., blue for without TB, red for with TB). Add data labels with the exact percentages on top of the bars. Console Report: Print the absolute numbers (N) of patients in each group and how many of them have HIV. Part 2: Clinical Pathway of HIV+ Patients (Sankey/Parallel Categories Diagram). Data Processing: Filter the dataset to include only patients who are HIV positive. Pathway Logic: For each HIV+ patient, determine their TB status (Positive/Negative) across all their records. Then, identify all medical specialties they were referred to (look for columns containing 'Para qual especialidade' and 'choice='). Destination Logic: Classify their final destination: If no referrals: 'Sem Encaminhamento (Resolvido na APS)'. If 1 referral: The name of that specialty. If >1 referral: 'Múltiplos Encaminhamentos (Histórico)'. Visualization: Create an interactive Plotly Parallel Categories diagram showing the flow: Coorte Base (HIV+) -> Diagnóstico de TB -> Destino Clínico. Color the flow paths based on co-infection risk (e.g., blue for HIV only, red for HIV+TB). Group less frequent specialties into an 'Outras Especialidades' category to keep the chart clean. Output 2 (Export & Print): Create a summary table that counts the exact number of unique patients for every path in the diagram. Print this table to the console and export it to a CSV named 'Tabela_Fluxo_Jornada_HIV_Pacientes_Unicos.csv'. Part 3: Age Distribution by CIAP (Ridgeline Plot for HIV+ Population). Data Processing: Filter for actual consultations (where 'Repeat Instrument' is not null) for HIV-positive patients. Ensure that 'Idade' (Age) and 'CIAP' are filled. You will need to propagate the age and HIV status from the initial registration rows to the consultation rows using forward fill grouped by 'Record ID'. Top Diagnoses: Identify the top 8 most frequent CIAP codes for this HIV+ population. Clean up the CIAP text strings so they aren't too long for the chart (truncate at 40 characters). Console Report: Print a ranking of the top 10 CIAP codes with their absolute counts and percentages. Visualization: Create a Ridgeline plot (using Plotly's Violin plot with side='positive') showing the age distribution for each of the top 8 CIAP codes. Use a sequential red color palette. Include the mean line in the violin plots. Output 3 (Export & Print): Generate a statistical summary table for these top 8 CIAP codes, including the count of consultations, minimum age, mean age, median age, and maximum age. Print this table to the console and export it to a CSV named 'Tabela_Ridgeline_Idades_CIAP_HIV.csv'. [cite: 1-25]

question: Comorbidity Profile: What is the prevalence of chronic diseases among people with a history of injecting drug use?
prompt: I need a comprehensive, 4-part advanced epidemiological and operational analysis in Python using a Telemedicine dataset (a CSV file named 'Table1'). Please use pandas, numpy, matplotlib, seaborn, plotly, scipy, and scikit-learn. For every part, you must generate a professional visualization, print a console report, and export the exact underlying data to a CSV file. Part 1: Unique Patients Analysis - Chronic Diseases vs. Injectable Drug Use. Data Processing: Identify Injectable Drug Users (UDVI), Hypertension (HAS), and Diabetes (DM) using both free-text mining (RegEx) and structured checkboxes. Aggregation (Gold Standard): Group by 'Record ID' to ensure the analysis is based on unique patients (1 patient = 1 row), keeping the maximum value if they ever had the condition. Statistics: Calculate the p-value using the Chi-Square test to see if there is a significant association between a history of injectable drug use and the prevalence of HAS/DM. Visualization: Create a clean, grouped bar chart (Seaborn) showing the prevalence (%) of HAS and DM, split by Injectable Drug history (Yes/No). Place the percentage labels directly on top of the bars. Output: Export the exact data used for the chart (including absolute cases, percentages, and p-values) to a CSV named 'Tabela_Prevalencia_Cronicas_Injetaveis_PACIENTES_UNICOS.csv'. Part 2: Resolution Flow (Cascade) and Time Distortion Analysis. Part 2A (Cascade Flow): Cross-reference the CIAP-2 diagnosis with the referred medical specialties. Classify the outcome as 'Resolvido na APS' (0 referrals) or 'Encaminhado' (1 or more referrals). Generate a Plotly Parallel Categories diagram tracking the Top 10 CIAPs to the Top 10 Destinations, colored by resolution status (Green for resolved, Red for referred). Export this flow to 'Tabela_Fluxo_CIAP_Resolutividade.csv'. Part 2B (Time Distortion): Extract numeric values (minutes) from the consultation duration column. Create a Seaborn Violin plot to visualize the distribution/distortion of consultation times, plotting vertical lines for both the Mean and Median. Generate a descriptive statistics table (Count, Mean, Std, Min, Quartiles, Max), translate the metric names to Portuguese, print it, and export to 'Tabela_Estatistica_Tempo_Atendimento.csv'. Part 3: Specific Referral Tracking (Ophthalmology vs. Endocrinology). Data Processing: Filter the dataset to include only consultations that resulted in a referral to Ophthalmology or Endocrinology. Visualization: Create a Plotly Parallel Categories diagram tracking the Top 15 CIAP codes specifically to these two specialties. Color the flow lines based on the destination specialty (e.g., Blue for Ophthalmology, Orange for Endocrinology). Output: Silently export the aggregated flow data to 'Tabela_Fluxo_Oftalmo_Endocrino.csv'. Part 4: AI NLP Clustering of Primary Care Outcomes for Superusers. Data Processing: Isolate consultations that did not result in a referral (resolved in Primary Care). AI Engine: Use Scikit-Learn (TfidfVectorizer and KMeans with 4 clusters) to analyze the free-text 'Desfecho/Conduta' (Outcome/Plan) column. Extract the top 3 keywords for each cluster to automatically name them. Superusers Filter: Filter the dataset to keep only the Top 10 'Record IDs' (Superusers) with the highest volume of consultations. Visualization: Create a Plotly Scatter/Bubble chart mapping 'Conduta / Desfecho (IA)' on the X-axis and 'Diagnóstico (CIAP-2)' on the Y-axis. Color the bubbles by the Superuser ID and set the bubble size to represent the volume of consultations. Adjust the layout margins so the long CIAP names fit on the screen. Output: Silently export the aggregated bubble chart data to 'Tabela_Dispersao_Superusuarios_IA.csv'. [cite: 26-50]

question: Aging and Health: How does the chronic disease burden evolve from age 45 onwards in this specific population?
prompt: I need a comprehensive Python analysis focused on chronic diseases and demographic factors using a Telemedicine dataset (a CSV file named 'Table1'). The analysis must be divided into three parts. For each part, generate a professional visualization, print a console report, and export the exact underlying data to a CSV file. Use pandas, numpy, matplotlib, seaborn, plotly, and scipy. Part 1: Evolution of Age by Chronic Disease (Ridgeline Plot). Data Processing: Focus on the 'Idade' (Age) column and all structured checkbox columns related to 'doenças crônicas' (chronic diseases). Aggregation: Propagate the age and chronic disease status across all patient records (forward fill) and keep only the last record per unique 'Record ID'. Melting & Cleaning: Reshape the data (melt) to have 'Doenca' and 'Status' columns. Keep only rows where 'Status' is 'Checked'. Clean the disease names (remove 'choice='). Crucially, exclude meaningless categories like 'não se aplica', 'nenhuma', 'outras', etc., using a RegEx exclusion pattern. Remove rows with missing age. Visualization: Select the Top 8 most frequent chronic diseases. Create an elegant Plotly Ridgeline Plot (Violin plot with side='positive') showing the age distribution for each disease. Set the background to solid white, use a bold qualitative color palette, add dark outlines to the violins, and include the mean line within each distribution. Reverse the order so the most frequent disease is at the top. Output: Generate a statistical summary table for these Top 8 diseases (Count, Min, Mean, Median, Max Age). Print it to the console and export it silently to 'Tabela_Ridgeline_Idades_DoencasCronicas.csv'. Part 2: Chronic Disease Burden and Aging (>45 years). Data Processing: Filter for actual consultations. Ensure 'Idade' is numeric and propagated correctly. Drop rows with missing age. Create a new column 'Grupo_Etario' (Age Group) classifying patients as '45 anos ou mais' (>= 45) or 'Menos de 45 anos' (< 45). Disease Tracking: Identify Hypertension (HAS) and Diabetes (DM) using both free-text mining (RegEx for terms like 'hipertensão', 'has', 'diabetes', 'dm2') and structured checkboxes. Combine these into binary columns ('Tem_HAS', 'Tem_DM'). Statistics: Use chi2_contingency from scipy.stats to calculate the p-value comparing the prevalence of HAS and DM between the two age groups. Output: Create a table containing the diagnosis (Hypertension/Diabetes), age group, total consultations, absolute cases, prevalence (%), and p-value. Print a detailed executive summary to the console showing these exact numbers and export the table silently to 'Tabela_Carga_Cronicas_Idade.csv'. Visualization: Create a Seaborn grouped bar chart showing the Prevalence (%) on the Y-axis and Diagnosis (HAS/DM) on the X-axis, grouped by Age Group. Use specific colors: Blue ('#4a90e2') for <45 and Terracotta ('#d55e52') for >=45. Add percentage labels directly on top of the bars and remove top/right spines. [cite: 51-71]

question: Sanitary Surveillance: What percentage of compulsory notification cases diagnosed via telemedicine effectively generated a SINAN number?
prompt: I need a two-part Python analysis to audit the filling of mandatory notification diseases (SINAN) in a Telemedicine dataset ('a CSV file named 'Table1'). Please use pandas, numpy, and plotly.express. Part 1: Clinical Audit & 100% Stacked Bar Chart (Minimalist Design). Data Processing: Dynamically find the column related to 'SINAN' or 'Notifica'. Then, build an expanded 'search engine' (a function) to categorize columns based on keywords for mandatory notification diseases (e.g., 'hiv', 'sífilis', 'tuberculose', 'dengue', 'violência', 'intoxicação'). Exclude columns containing terms like 'exame', 'teste', 'suspeita'. Aggregation: Group the data by 'Record ID' to ensure we track unique patients. If a patient ever had a confirmed disease and ever had a SINAN number filled out (not empty, 'nan', '0', 'não'), record that. Visualization: Create a horizontal 100% stacked bar chart using Plotly Express (px.bar). The Y-axis should be the Disease names, sorted by the total number of cases (highest volume at the bottom of the chart so they draw attention). The X-axis should be the Percentage (from 0 to 100%). The colors must be exactly '#d9534f' (Red) for 'Não (Falta Notificar)' and '#5cb85c' (Green) for 'Sim (Notificado na Base)'. Ensure Red comes first (left). Design Requirements: The chart must be highly minimalist. Remove the X-axis completely (lines, numbers, titles), remove the Y-axis line and grid. Place the text inside the bars, anchored to the end (insidetextanchor='end'), showing both the absolute number of cases and the percentage (e.g., '10 casos (20.0%)') in white Arial Black font. Put the legend horizontally at the top. Output: Export the aggregated data (Disease, Notification Status, Number of Patients, Total Cases of Disease, Proportion %) to a CSV named 'Tabela_Auditoria_SINAN_Ampliada.csv'. Part 2: Executive Summary Report (Console Output). Using the same data processing logic as above, calculate the total number of confirmed cases of mandatory notification diseases, the number of cases with a SINAN number recorded, the number of blank cases, and the overall 'Effectiveness Rate' (Taxa de Efetividade de Registro) as a percentage. Print a formatted executive summary to the console displaying these numbers. Add an automated management alert (⚠️ ALERTA DE GESTÃO) if the effectiveness rate is below 80%, recommending system locks (mandatory fields) to prevent closing records without the SINAN number. [cite: 72-86]

question: Operational Efficiency - Consultation Time: What is the average duration of tele-appointments by specialty, and which demand the most physician time?
prompt: I need an advanced data analysis and data-cleaning script in Python for a Telemedicine dataset (CSV file named 'Table1'). Use pandas, numpy, matplotlib, seaborn, and regular expressions (re). Part 1: Fail-Safe Time Column Detection and Cleaning. Build a robust 'fail-safe' detector function to identify the true column that stores the consultation time ('tempo', 'duração', 'minutos') while ignoring columns related to 'especialidade'. The function must handle messy data: extract numbers even if mixed with text (like 'h' or 'hora'), convert hours to minutes, and ignore empty strings, 'nan', 'unchecked', or '0'. Test candidate columns and select the one with the highest count of valid numeric time entries. Print a diagnostic message identifying the chosen column and its valid count. Calculate a Quality Indicator: The percentage of consultations that actually have the time filled in versus left blank. Print a management alert if more than 20% are blank. Part 2: Specialty Cross-Referencing (Real Data vs. Fallback). Cross-reference the valid time data with the medical specialties ('Para qual especialidade', 'choice='). For each consultation, match the filled time with the specific specialty requested and the CIAP-2 code. Fallback Mechanism: If the initial cross-reference yields an empty dataset (meaning time and specialty were filled in different forms/instances), automatically activate a 'Data Cascade' (forward fill grouped by 'Record ID') to align the data longitudinally and try the extraction again. Raise a fatal error if it still fails. Part 3: Outlier Mathematics (IQR Method). Apply the Interquartile Range (IQR) mathematical method to calculate the lower and upper bounds of normal consultation times. Separate the data into 'df_normal' (clean data) and 'df_outliers'. Calculate both the 'dirty mean' (with outliers) and the 'clean mean' (without outliers). Part 4: Visualization (Clean Violin Plot). Create a Seaborn Violin Plot showing the distribution of 'Tempo de Consulta (Minutos)' on the X-axis for the Top 10 most frequent specialties on the Y-axis. Crucially, use only the clean data without outliers (df_normal). The violins should use the 'plasma_r' color palette, be semi-transparent, and not show the inner boxplot. Overlay a scatterplot (stripplot) of the actual data points in black, and add a red diamond marker representing the mean for each specialty. Customize the Y-axis labels to include the specialty name (truncated at 25 characters), the sample size (n), and the exact median time in minutes. Sort the Y-axis descending by median time. Part 5: Exporting Data and Auditing Outliers. Export the exact statistical data used to build the chart (Count, Min, Mean, Median, Max, Standard Deviation for the clean data of the Top 10 specialties) to a CSV named 'Tabela_Estatistica_Tempo_Especialidade.csv'. Print a detailed Executive Report to the console. It must include the Quality Indicator results, the Outlier Audit (comparing the clean vs. dirty mean), and specifically list the Top 3 Specialties and Top 3 CIAP diagnoses that caused the extremely long outlier consultations (those above the upper IQR limit). [cite: 87-103]

question: First Contact Resolution: What is the proportion of patients who resolve their demand in the first teleconsultation?
prompt: I need a Python script to analyze the 'Non-Resolution' reasons in the first contact of a Telemedicine service, using the dataset a CSV file named 'Table1'. The analysis must be divided into three parts. Use pandas, numpy, matplotlib, seaborn, and plotly. Part 1: Text Mining for Non-Resolution Reasons (Bar Chart). Data Processing: Search the free-text clinical notes columns (like 'conduta', 'evolucao', 'plano') for specific keywords that indicate the issue was not resolved in the primary care consultation. The categories and keywords are: 'Solicitação de Exames' (Keywords: 'exame', 'laborat', 'sangue', 'rx', 'raio-x', 'ultrassom', 'solicito'); 'Encaminhamento Presencial' (Keywords: 'presencial', 'ubs', 'upa', 'exame fisico', 'palpação'); 'Encaminhamento Especialista' (Keywords: 'especialista', 'encaminho', 'parecer', 'cardiologista'); 'Retorno Agendado' (Keywords: 'retorno', 'voltar', 'dias', 'reavaliar'). Visualization: Create a horizontal Bar Chart (Seaborn) showing the frequency of these 4 categories, sorted from highest to lowest. Use the 'flare' color palette. Display the absolute values directly at the end of each bar. Remove the top and right spines of the chart. Output: Print an executive diagnostic identifying the top limiting factor. Export a table with the Reason, Frequency, and Proportion (%) to a CSV file. Part 2: Funnel Classification and Sankey Diagram. Data Processing: Implement a decision funnel (hierarchy) to classify the 'Desfecho' (Outcome) of each consultation into one of the 4 non-resolution categories or 'Resolvido/Alta' (Resolved/Discharged). The priority order for classification should be: 1. Presencial, 2. Especialista, 3. Exames, 4. Retorno. If none match, classify as 'Resolvido/Alta'. Ignore entries with 'não compareceu' or very short text (<5 chars) as 'Outros'. Calculations: Calculate the total number of valid consultations, the number of 'Resolved' cases, and the number of 'Unresolved' cases (which is the sum of the 4 non-resolution categories). Visualization: Create a Sankey Diagram using Plotly to visualize this 'Mass Balance' flow. Node 0: Total Atendimentos; Node 1: Resolvido/Alta; Node 2: Não Resolvido; Nodes 3-6: The specific non-resolution reasons (Retorno, Exames, Especialista, Presencial). Include the absolute numbers (N) in the node labels. Output: Print a validation check in the terminal to ensure the numbers sum up correctly (Total = Resolved + Unresolved). Part 3: Generating the Sankey Base Data Table. Recreate the logic from Part 2 to establish the exact numerical values for the flow: 'Total' branching into 'Resolved' and 'Unresolved', and then 'Unresolved' branching into the 4 specific reasons. Output: Create a pandas DataFrame structured specifically for generating Sankey diagrams (Source, Target, Value). Print this table to the console and export the table as a CSV file named 'Table1.csv'. [cite: 114-127]

question: Super-Users: Analysis of hyper-utilizers and clinical chapters.
prompt: I need a Python script to analyze 'Superusers' (hyper-utilizers) in a Telemedicine dataset (CSV file named 'Table1'). Use pandas, matplotlib, seaborn, and plotly. Define 'Superusers' as patients in the 95th percentile (P95) for total number of consultations. Part 1: Superuser Profile Dashboard. Data Processing: Filter out no-shows ('não compareceu' or 'falta'). Calculate the total number of consultations per unique 'Record ID'. Determine the P95 threshold. Filter the dataset to include only consultations belonging to these Superusers. Clinical Mapping: Map the CIAP-2 codes to their main chapters (e.g., 'A' to 'Geral/Inespecífico', 'P' to 'Psicológico') using a dictionary. Visualization: Create a 1x2 figure. Left side: A Histogram showing the distribution of total consultations per patient across the entire valid dataset. Add a vertical red dashed line indicating the P95 threshold. Right side: A horizontal Bar chart showing the Top 8 most frequent CIAP-2 chapters specifically for the Superuser group. Use the 'viridis' palette. Output: Print a support table for the article containing the CIAP Chapter, Total Consultations, and Proportion (%). Export this table silently to 'tabela_superusuarios.csv'. Part 2: Hyper-utilization Flow (Sankey Diagram). Data Processing: Using the same Superuser dataset, calculate the frequency of each CIAP-2 chapter label. Create a 'Source-Target-Value' DataFrame needed for a Sankey diagram. The flow should be: Link 1: 'Superusuários (P95)' -> 'Total de Consultas'; Link 2: 'Total de Consultas' -> [Each specific CIAP-2 Chapter]. Visualization: Create a Plotly Sankey Diagram. Map the string labels to numeric indices. Format the node labels to include the exact N (number of individuals or number of consultations) and the percentage for the CIAP nodes. Use the color '#e67e22' for the nodes and semi-transparent links. Output: Print the underlying Source-Target-Value table to the console and export it to a CSV named 'base_sankey_hiperutilizacao.csv'. [cite: 128-134]

question: Superuser Profile: Dynamic identification and clinical mapping.
prompt: Act as an expert Python Data Scientist and Epidemiologist. I need a robust Python script to analyze 'Superusers' (hyper-utilizers) in a Telemedicine dataset. Environment & Setup: File: Read from a CSV named Table1 using low_memory=False. Libraries: Use pandas, numpy, matplotlib.pyplot, seaborn, and plotly.graph_objects. Robustness: Do not hardcode exact column names. Dynamically identify the required columns (Patient ID, CIAP/Diagnosis, and Outcome/Status) using partial string matching (e.g., searching for 'ciap', 'desfecho', 'status' in df.columns). Definition of a Superuser: A Superuser is defined as a patient whose total number of valid consultations falls in or above the 95th percentile (P95) of the entire patient population. Please divide the code into two main sections: Part 1: Superuser Profile Dashboard (Matplotlib & Seaborn). Data Cleaning: Filter out any 'no-shows' (e.g., rows where the status contains 'não compareceu' or 'falta'). P95 Calculation: Group by Patient ID to calculate the total number of consultations per patient. Determine the P95 threshold. Create a filtered DataFrame containing only the consultations of these Superusers. Clinical Mapping (CIAP-2): Extract the first letter of the CIAP-2 code and map it to its main chapter using a dictionary (e.g., 'A': 'Geral/Inespecífico', 'P': 'Psicológico', 'R': 'Respiratório', etc.). If the code is missing or invalid, map it to 'Outros Diagnósticos'. Visualization (1x2 Figure): Left Subplot (Histogram): Plot the distribution of total consultations per patient for the entire valid dataset using sns.histplot. Add a prominent vertical red dashed line indicating the exact P95 threshold. Right Subplot (Horizontal Bar Chart): Plot the Top 8 most frequent CIAP-2 chapters specifically for the Superuser cohort using the viridis palette. Output: Print a clean summary table to the console showing the CIAP Chapter, Total Consultations, and Proportion (%) for the superusers. Silently export this exact table to a CSV named tabela_superusuarios.csv. Part 2: Hyper-utilization Flow (Plotly Sankey Diagram). Flow Logic: Using the Superuser dataset, calculate the frequency of each mapped CIAP-2 chapter. Create a 'Source-Target-Value' DataFrame to map the flow of clinical demand. Sankey Structure: Link 1: 'Superusuários (P95)' -> 'Total de Consultas' (Value = total number of superuser consultations). Link 2: 'Total de Consultas' -> [Each specific CIAP-2 Chapter] (Value = frequency of that chapter). Visualization (Plotly): Generate a Sankey diagram. Map the string labels to numeric indices automatically. Custom Labels: Format the node labels to display the exact N (e.g., 'SUPERUSUÁRIOS (N=50 Indivíduos)'). For the CIAP nodes, display the absolute count and the percentage. Styling: Use the color #e67e22 (orange) for the nodes and a semi-transparent version rgba(230, 126, 34, 0.3) for the links. Output: Print the underlying Source-Target-Value DataFrame to the console for validation, and export it to a CSV named base_sankey_hiperutilizacao.csv. [cite: 135-153]

question: Gender Identity: Relationship between gender and clinical complexity.
prompt: Act as an expert Python Data Scientist and Epidemiologist. I need a robust Python script to analyze the relationship between 'Gender Identity' and 'Clinical Complexity' in a Telemedicine dataset. Environment & Setup: File: Read from a CSV named Table1 using low_memory=False. Libraries: Use pandas, numpy, matplotlib.pyplot, and seaborn. Robustness: Dynamically identify columns using partial string matching for Gender Identity ('identidade de gênero'), Biological Sex ('sexo'), Infectious Diseases, Chronic Diseases, Specialties, and CIAP-2. Please divide the code into the following sections: Part 1: Data Cleaning & Smart Gender Imputation. Longitudinal Fill: Sort by Patient ID and Instance, then forward-fill the demographic and clinical columns so history is preserved across consultations. Smart Imputation: Create a function to infer the patient's group. If the 'Gender Identity' field is missing, 'NaN', or 'Prefers not to say', infer it from the 'Biological Sex' column (e.g., Male -> 'Homem Cis', Female -> 'Mulher Cis'). If explicitly stated, map them correctly to 'Mulher Cis', 'Homem Cis', 'Mulher Trans', and 'Homem Trans'. Drop unmapped rows. Complexity Calculation: Calculate the 'Total Disease Burden' per consultation by summing all marked infectious and chronic disease checkboxes. Part 2: Specialty Filtering & Exporting. Melting: Melt the Specialties columns. Targeting: Identify the Top 3 most requested specialties for each gender group. Combine these into a unique target list, and explicitly force the inclusion of 'Psiquiatria/Saúde Mental' (Psychiatry) in this list if it isn't there already. Outputs: Export a statistical summary (Mean, Std, Count of Disease Burden grouped by Specialty and Gender) to base_complexidade_genero.csv. Export the Top 5 CIAP-2 reasons for each gender (with absolute frequencies and proportions) to base_ciap_por_genero.csv. Print the Top 3 CIAP-2 reasons per gender to the console. Part 3: Visualization. Create a large horizontal grouped Bar Plot (sns.barplot). Axes: Specialty on the Y-axis, Total Disease Burden on the X-axis. Group by Gender Identity (hue). Styling: Use a custom palette: Cis Women (Gray #95a5a6), Cis Men (Dark Blue #34495e), Trans Women (Purple #9b59b6), Trans Men (Orange #e67e22). Add error bars (capsize=.1). Remove top and right spines. [cite: 154-172]

question: Access by Race: Comparative analysis of infectious vs. chronic burdens.
prompt: Act as an expert Python Data Scientist and Epidemiologist. I need a robust Python script to perform a comparative analysis of the epidemiological profile by 'Race/Color' (IBGE Standard) regarding Infectious vs. Chronic disease burdens. Environment & Setup: File: Read from a CSV named Table1 using low_memory=False. Libraries: Use pandas, numpy, matplotlib.pyplot, and seaborn. Robustness: Do not hardcode exact column names. Dynamically identify columns using partial string matching for Race/Ethnicity (search for 'raça' or 'etnia'), Infectious Diseases, Chronic Diseases, and CIAP-2. Please divide the code into the following sections: Part 1: IBGE Race Mapping & Data Prep. Longitudinal Fill: Sort by Patient ID (Record ID) and Instance, then forward-fill the race and disease columns so the history is preserved. Keep only actual valid consultations. Flexible Mapping: Create a robust function to clean and map the free-text race inputs into the 5 official IBGE categories: 'Branca', 'Preta', 'Parda', 'Amarela', 'Indígena'. Account for variations (e.g., 'branco', 'indigena'). Drop any unmapped rows. Separate Burdens: Binarize the disease checkbox columns. Calculate the 'Infectious Burden' and 'Chronic Burden' as two separate numerical columns by summing their respective checked boxes per consultation. Part 2: Exporting & Console Reports. Melting: Melt the dataframe to compare 'Tipo de Doença' (Infectious vs. Chronic) against the 'Quantidade de Doenças' (Burden). Outputs: Export a statistical summary table (Mean, Std, N) grouped by Race and Disease Profile to base_carga_doencas_raca.csv. Ensure the table follows the official IBGE categorical order. Export the Top 5 CIAP-2 consultation motives per race (with N and Proportions) to base_ciap_por_raca.csv. Print a clean numeric synthesis of the means and the Top 3 CIAP-2 reasons per race to the terminal. Part 3: Visualization. Create a large, clean grouped Bar Plot using Seaborn (sns.barplot). Axes: Place 'Raça/Cor (IBGE)' on the X-axis and the 'Média de Doenças' on the Y-axis. Group the bars by Disease Type (hue). Styling: Use a diverging color palette to contrast the conditions: Infectious Burden (Red #e74c3c) and Chronic Burden (Blue #2980b9). Include 95% Confidence Interval error bars (capsize=.05). Remove the top and right spines of the chart to give it an academic, minimalist look. [cite: 209-224]

question: Mental Health: Correlation between poly-substance use and psychiatric referral.
prompt: Act as an expert Python Data Scientist and Psychiatrist. I need a highly advanced Python script to analyze Mental Health, Chemical Dependency (Dual Pathology), Poly-substance use, and Psychiatric referral queues in a Telemedicine dataset. Environment & Setup: File: Read from a CSV named Table1 using low_memory=False. Libraries: Use pandas, numpy, matplotlib.pyplot, seaborn, and scipy.stats. Robustness: Dynamically identify columns using partial string matching for the Psychiatry referral column ('psiquiatria', 'especialidade'), CIAP-2, and drugs. Please divide the code into three distinct phases (cells): Phase 1: Poly-substance Use & Statistical Correlation. Data Prep: Dynamically track target drugs (Crack, Alcohol, Cannabis, Cocaine, Tobacco, Other) looking for 'choice=Sim', and locate the Psychiatry referral column. Sort by Patient ID and Instance, then forward-fill longitudinally. Keep only valid consultations. Metrics: Calculate a 'Poly-substance Index' (sum of drugs used per consultation) and binarize the Psychiatry referral (1 = Yes, 0 = No). Exporting: Aggregate the data by the Poly-substance Index to calculate the Total Consultations and the Psychiatry Referral Rate (%). Export this to base_poliuso_psiquiatria.csv. Statistics: Perform a Point-Biserial Correlation test (scipy.stats.pointbiserialr) between the Poly-substance Index and the Psychiatry Referral flag. Export the correlation coefficient (r), p-value, and N to base_estatistica_poliuso.csv. Visualization & Report: Create a combined chart: A Bar chart showing the Psychiatry Referral Rate (%) on the primary Y-axis, and a Line chart showing the Absolute Volume of Consultations on a secondary Y-axis. Print the statistical results to the console, including a clinical management interpretation if the p-value is significant (< 0.05). Phase 2: Dual Pathology & Patient-Level Profiling. Mining: Use Regex to mine free-text fields for drugs (crack, cocaína, heroína, opioide, maconha/cannabis) and capture structured checkboxes for legal drugs (alcohol, tobacco). Also locate the Psychiatry referral column. Patient Aggregation: Group by Patient ID (Record ID) using .max() to create a lifetime history per unique patient (1 row = 1 patient). Profiling: Classify patients into 4 clinical profiles based on usage: 'Não Usuário', 'Usuário Simples (1 Droga Leve)', 'Poliusuário (Lícitas/Leves)', and 'Usuário de Drogas Pesadas' (if they used crack, cocaine, heroin, or opioids). Create a separate flag for 'Status_Crack'. Outputs: Export the substance ranking to base_ranking_substancias.csv, the clinical profile vs psychiatry rates to base_perfil_drogas_psiquiatria.csv, and the Crack factor vs psychiatry rates to base_fator_crack_psiquiatria.csv. Dashboard & Odds: Create a 3-panel matplotlib figure. Top (spanning both columns): Horizontal Bar chart ranking the most used substances (highlight heavy drugs in red). Bottom Left: Bar chart showing the 'Cascade Effect' (Referral rate by Clinical Profile). Bottom Right: Bar chart showing the 'Crack Factor' (Referral rate of non-users vs. Crack users). Print a report calculating the risk multipliers (Odds) for Poly-users and heavy drug users compared to non-users, with a special focus on the Crack multiplier. Phase 3: Psychiatry X-Ray (Isolating the Queue). Filtering: Filter the dataset to keep only the consultations where the patient was actually referred to Psychiatry. Exporting: Count the absolute frequencies and proportions (%) of every CIAP-2 reason present in this specific queue. Export the complete list to base_motivos_psiquiatria.csv. Visualization: Isolate the Top 7 CIAP-2 reasons (truncate text at 65 chars). Create a horizontal Bar Plot. Highlight the absolute #1 leading cause in vibrant red (#e74c3c), and color the rest in gray (#bdc3c7). Annotate the bars with exact N and %. Remove top and right spines. Report: Print an executive summary identifying the #1 offender. If this top reason contains 'ansiedade' or 'depressivo' (Common Mental Disorders), print a targeted management alert advising that these cases have high potential for resolution in Primary Care with proper training. [cite: 225-249]

question: Respiratory Seasonality: Correlation between respiratory diseases and climate temperature.
prompt: Act as an expert Python Data Scientist and Epidemiologist. I need a robust Python script to perform a time-series analysis correlating the seasonality of Respiratory Diseases with the average climate temperature in a Telemedicine dataset. Environment & Setup: File: Read from a CSV named TELESAPFormulrioMdic-EXPORTAOBOLSISTAAtua_DATA_LABELS_2025-07-23_1329.csv using low_memory=False. Libraries: Use pandas, numpy, matplotlib.pyplot, seaborn, and scipy.stats.chisquare. Robustness: Dynamically identify the date/timestamp column. Handle missing dates by dropping invalid rows. Dynamically identify text columns and checkboxes for respiratory conditions. Please divide the code into four distinct phases: Phase 1: Time-Series Extraction & Clinical Mining. Temporal Parsing: Dynamically locate the column containing 'data', 'timestamp', or 'date'. Convert it to datetime and extract the numerical Month. Respiratory Mining: Identify cases of Asthma, COPD, or Bronchitis. Use a hybrid approach: Text Mining: Search free-text columns using regex (e.g., \b(asma|dpoc|bronquite|doença pulmonar obstrutiva)\b). Structured Data: Search checkbox columns containing 'asma' or 'dpoc'. Binarization: Unify the findings into a single binary column Caso_Respiratorio (1 if any condition is met, 0 otherwise). Phase 2: Climate Integration & Statistical Testing. Monthly Aggregation: Group the data by Month (1 to 12). Calculate the Total Consultations, the Absolute Number of Respiratory Cases, and the % Rate of Respiratory Cases per month. Ensure all 12 months are represented (fill missing months with 0). Climate Mapping: Hardcode a dictionary with the historical average temperatures for São Paulo for each month (Jan: 23.1, Feb: 23.5, Mar: 22.5, Apr: 21.2, May: 18.4, Jun: 17.5, Jul: 16.6, Aug: 17.7, Sep: 19.3, Oct: 20.5, Nov: 21.6, Dec: 22.4) and map it to the aggregated dataframe. Chi-Square Test: Perform a Chi-Square test (chisquare) comparing the observed respiratory cases against the expected cases (assuming a uniform distribution across the total volume). Determine if the seasonal variance is statistically significant (p < 0.05). Phase 3: Data Export. Export 1: Export the monthly seasonal dataframe (Month, Total Consultations, Respiratory Cases, % Rate, Average SP Temp) to base_sazonalidade_respiratoria.csv. Export 2: Export the exact results of the Chi-Square test (Total N, Chi-Square Stat, P-Value, and Text Interpretation) to base_estatistica_sazonalidade.csv. Phase 4: Dual-Axis Visualization & Executive Report. Visualization: Create a Dual-Axis chart (ax1 and ax1.twinx()). Primary Y-Axis (Bar Chart): Plot the % Rate of Respiratory Cases as vertical bars in Blue (#3498db). Annotate the exact percentages on top of the bars. Secondary Y-Axis (Line Chart): Plot the Average SP Temperature as a Line with markers in Red (#e74c3c). Crucially, force the Y-limit of this secondary axis to start at 0 and end at 30 to provide a stable visual scale. Add the Statistical interpretation and p-value dynamically into the chart title. Color the title text Red if significant, Gray if not. Console Report: Print an Epidemiological Synthesis to the terminal. Include the Total N, total respiratory episodes, and the statistical diagnosis. If the variation is significant (p < 0.05), trigger a 'Management Alert' indicating the peak month, its proportion of the demand, and the climate temperature of that specific month.

question: Referral Bottlenecks: Specialty queue weight and epidemiological dossiers.
prompt: Act as an expert Python Data Scientist and Healthcare Operations Analyst. I need a highly advanced Python script to analyze specialty referral bottlenecks and generate comprehensive epidemiological dossiers for specific medical queues in a Telemedicine dataset. Environment & Setup: File: Read from a CSV named Table1 using low_memory=False. Libraries: Use pandas, numpy, matplotlib.pyplot, seaborn, and warnings. Robustness: Dynamically identify columns using partial string matching for specialties ('encaminhamento', 'especialidade'), CIAP-2, age, sex, infectious diseases, and chronic diseases. Please divide the code into three distinct phases: Phase 1: Referral Bottlenecks (Queue Weight vs. Patient Risk). Data Prep: Isolate all specialty referral columns and binarize them (1 if checked, 0 otherwise). Clean the column names for readability. Metrics: For the Top 5 most requested specialties, calculate: Visão A (Queue Weight): The proportion of referrals each specialty represents out of the total volume of generated referrals. Visão B (Patient Risk): The percentage of unique individuals (Record ID) who required a referral to that specialty. Outputs: Export the consolidated demand data to base_demanda_especialidades.csv. Audit the Top 5 specialties to find their Top 5 CIAP-2 motives and export this clinical audit to base_auditoria_ciap_especialidades.csv. Visualization: Create a 1x2 side-by-side Bar chart dashboard plotting Visão A and Visão B. Use a custom conditional palette: highlight 'Psiquiatria' (Psychiatry) in vibrant red (#e74c3c) and color all other specialties in neutral gray (#bdc3c7). Annotate bars with exact percentages. Phase 2: Mental Health Epidemiological Dossier. Data Prep: Isolate the queue of patients referred to Psychiatry/Mental Health. Forward and backward fill their demographic data (Age, Sex) and CIAP-2. Clinical Mapping: Calculate the total physical burden by summing infectious and chronic diseases. Use text mining (regex) and checkboxes to map substance use (Crack, Cocaine, Marijuana, Heroin, Opioids, Alcohol, Tobacco). Outputs: Export 4 specific databases for this queue: CIAP-2 Motives (base_dossie_motivos_psiq.csv), Demographics (base_dossie_demografia_psiq.csv), Comorbidities (base_dossie_comorbidades_psiq.csv), Substance Use (base_dossie_substancias_psiq.csv). Visualization: Create a large 2x2 Dashboard: Panel 1 (Motives): Horizontal bar chart of Top 5 CIAP-2 codes (Reds_r palette). Panel 2 (Demographics): Violin plot of Age distribution by Sex (muted palette). Panel 3 (Comorbidities): Grouped bar chart comparing Infectious vs. Chronic burden. Panel 4 (Chemical Map): Horizontal bar chart ranking substance use (viridis palette). Phase 3: Dynamic Specialty Dossiers (Ophthalmology & Orthopedics). Automation: Replicate the exact dossier generation logic from Phase 2, but wrap it in a dynamic loop targeting ['oftalmologia', 'ortopedia']. Outputs: For each target in the loop, automatically calculate the cohort metrics and export the 4 respective CSV files (e.g., base_dossie_motivos_oftalmologia.csv, base_dossie_demografia_ortopedia.csv, etc.). Visualization: Generate the 2x2 dashboard for each specialty. Dynamically change the primary highlight color of the dashboard based on the specialty being processed (e.g., use Blue #2980b9 for Ophthalmology and Green #27ae60 for Orthopedics). Print a console verification for ages and CIAP-2 motives per specialty. [cite: 250-271]

question: Cardiovascular Risk: Stratification and patient profile analysis.
prompt: Act as an expert Python Data Scientist and Public Health Analyst. I need a robust Python script to perform a Cardiovascular Risk (RCV) stratification analysis on a Telemedicine dataset. Environment & Setup: File: Read from a CSV named Table1 using low_memory=False. Libraries: Use pandas, numpy, matplotlib.pyplot, and seaborn. Robustness: Dynamically identify text columns and structured checkbox columns for chronic diseases. Protect against NaN values during text mining. Please divide the code into four distinct phases: Phase 1: Clinical Text Mining and Patient Consolidation. Feature Engineering: Track three specific comorbidities: Hypertension (HAS), Diabetes (DM), and Dyslipidemia/Cholesterol (DLP). Hybrid Mining: For each disease, check both the free-text clinical notes using Regex (e.g., \b(hipertens[aã]o|has)\b, \b(diabetes|dm2)\b, \b(colesterol|dlp)\b) and the structured 'Doenças Crônicas' checkbox columns. Create three binary columns (Tem_HAS, Tem_DM, Tem_DLP). Consolidation: Group the data by unique Patient ID (Record ID) using .max() so that if a patient ever presented the comorbidity in their lifetime history, they are flagged as 1. Phase 2: Cardiovascular Risk (RCV) Stratification. Clinical Logic: Create a new column Estratificacao_RCV classifying each unique patient into one of three tiers based on the following medical rules: Alto Risco (High Risk): Patient has Diabetes (DM) OR presents a combination of Hypertension + Dyslipidemia (HAS + DLP). Risco Moderado (Moderate Risk): Patient has isolated Hypertension (HAS) OR isolated Dyslipidemia (DLP), but no Diabetes. Baixo Risco (Low Risk): Patient has none of the mapped factors. Phase 3: Data Export. Export 1 (Stratification): Count the absolute number and calculate the proportion (%) of patients in each of the 3 risk tiers. Export this to base_estatificacao_rcv.csv. Export 2 (High-Risk Profile): Isolate the 'Alto Risco' cohort and calculate its internal composition: how many patients are there due to Diabetes vs. how many are there purely due to the dangerous HAS + DLP combination. Export this breakdown to base_perfil_alto_risco_rcv.csv. Phase 4: Visualization & Executive Report. Visualization: Create a horizontal Bar chart (sns.barplot) mapping the Cardiovascular Risk Level on the Y-axis and the Number of Unique Patients on the X-axis. Styling: Use a 'traffic light' color palette reflecting clinical urgency: Red (#c0392b) for High Risk, Orange (#f39c12) for Moderate Risk, and Green (#2ecc71) for Low Risk. Add exact N and % annotations directly on the bars. Remove the top and right spines. Console Report: Print an executive diagnostic report to the terminal summarizing the population's RCV. Include a specific sub-section detailing the clinical profile of the High-Risk group (Total Diabetics vs. Total HAS+DLP combinations). [cite: 272-286]
